text: The surgeon disassembles the ileostomy, dividing it into two sections, with one section being prepared for rhinostomosis.
The surgeon sections the mesentery near the intestine, releasing the terminal ileum and cecum from surrounding tissue.

The surgeon releases the cecum from adhesions formed during previous surgery using bipolar cautery, cold scissors, and blunt dissection. The adhesions between the vaginal dome and the cecum are completely separated, and the right ureter is identified.
The surgeon uses bipolar scissors and Roma dilution to complete the urethrolysis, fully separating the blind from the ureter.
The surgeon carefully separates the cecum, opening the peritoneal leaves that hold it, ensuring the ureter remains free and uninvolved.
The surgeon releases the parietal peritoneum and rectum, fully freeing the cecum for extirpation. The close work ensures the vascularization of the remaining intestine remains intact.
The surgeon identifies the cleavage plane and places a mechanical suture, followed by the complete sectioning of the ascending colon.

The surgeon observes the change in coloration between the tissue that will be stretched and the tissue that will remain for reanastomosis. The surgical piece is completely separated and extracted through the heliostomy opening.

The surgeon performs an incision at the level of the ascending colon and another at the terminal ileum, using a previously placed mark from the heliostomy as a guide. A linear groove is placed at the incision sites.
The surgeon performs the reanastomosis of the ileum with the ascending colon by aligning and suturing the openings made in each section.
The surgeon completes the ileocolic resection by performing an intracorporeal suture to close the remaining gap, using a return and return technique to ensure secure closure.
